name: Sequence Memory
description: The player observes or discovers an ordered sequence, then repeats it through buttons, symbols, notes, or interactions.
design_notes: Sequence memory puzzles need explicit input_sequence_id data, current puzzle_step_index, wrong-input policy, replay or hint options, and checkpoint behavior. The puzzle should test recall or deduction, not whether players can perceive inaccessible cues.
design_purpose: Challenge players to observe, remember, and reproduce an ordered pattern.
Gate rewards or routes behind readable clues distributed through a room or narrative context.
Support audio, visual, symbol, or spatial memory in compact puzzle setups.
player_fantasy: The player feels attentive and clever when they recall the pattern and enter it correctly.

parameters.name: input_sequence_id
parameters.description: Stable sequence definition used by clues, input validation, and save state.

parameters.name: puzzle_step_index
parameters.description: Current accepted position within the sequence.

parameters.name: activation_order
parameters.typical_range: red, blue, green or button ids
parameters.description: Ordered interactable ids, symbols, notes, or colors that solve the puzzle.

parameters.name: wrong_input_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule applied after a player enters an incorrect sequence step.

parameters.name: hint_stage_id
parameters.description: Assistance stage available through difficulty, repeated failure, or accessibility settings.

parameters.name: sequence_entry_window_ms
parameters.description: Optional interval allowed between accepted steps before the sequence resets.

edge_cases: A player presses two inputs during the same frame or network tick.
The sequence clue is visible only once and the player reloads after seeing half of it.
Wrong input should reset the step index but not reset other solved room state.
Co-op players press different sequence buttons nearly simultaneously.
common_bugs: The displayed replay sequence differs from activation_order after localization or content edits.
Step index is saved after input but before the success event, causing repeated final-step rewards.
Audio-only cues fail for players with muted sound because no alternate clue path exists.
Input lockout blocks interactions but the UI still accepts hover or focus states as if active.
balancing_notes: Longer sequences should be paired with stronger clue repetition or chunking.
Wrong-input penalties should match the puzzle tone; harsh resets are poor fits for long memory chains.
If the puzzle is required for progression, hints should escalate before players abandon the route.
accessibility_notes: Provide non-audio alternatives for sound sequences and non-color alternatives for color sequences.
Offer replay, notes, slower presentation, or hint-stage options for players with memory or perception differences.

implementation_notes.unity: Store input_sequence_id, puzzle_step_index, hint_stage_id, and solved state in a puzzle state component. Interactable buttons should submit ids to one validator so animation, audio, and success events cannot diverge.
implementation_notes.godot: Use a Resource for activation_order and a node or autoload store for runtime step state. Signals from buttons should be queued and resolved in order, with UI hints reading from the same sequence data.
implementation_notes.unreal: An Actor Component can validate input ids and replicate step state for co-op rooms. Gate repeated final-step events with a solved flag and use server authority if multiple players can input.
implementation_notes.web: Keep sequence validation in a small deterministic model and render cues from that model. Debounce pointer and keyboard events through input ids so rapid clicks cannot skip or double-count steps.

example_games.title: The Witness
example_games.note: Uses observation and pattern recall across puzzle spaces.

example_games.title: Simon
example_games.note: Uses repeated ordered input as the central memory challenge.
